In case an application component is showing a considerable high data footprint in your situation and does not show up in the above list, you can

  • Use SAP Joule or your personal favorite AI.
  • Search in SAP for Me with the component name. In SAP for Me you will find related SAP Notes for this component and in the header of a randomly picked Note, the description text of the application component can be seen:

 

  • Check if an ILM object related to the component appears in ILM Advisor. If the application component is larger than 30 GB and this is a component related to business data and there is no relatable ILM object appearing in ILM Advisor, please open an incident on component BC-ILM-ADV for further analysis.
  • SAP’s Foundational Success Plan provides the guidance, content, and empowerment you need to define a Data Volume Management (DVM) roadmap to stabilize system growth and reduce existing database size. Available remote services can be requested via the ‘Get Support’ application in SAP for Me (see SAP Note 1296527)

Conclusion

Before going into details of possible actions to take, let us summarize the steps to take to gain insights regarding the drivers for the HANA memory consumption of your tenant:

  1. Check if there is a concern regarding growing HANA memory consumption and consequently growing license consumption of the HANA memory related license metrics. Check license metrics C414, CY263, CY500 (only one is relevant depending on your contract) in the License Dashboard in SAP for Me.
  2. Check HANA Memory Analysis app to see if memory growth is caused by growth or data footprint or by extensive memory usage by query processing
  3. If query processing could be a reason, check app SQL Statement Analysis. In case of SAP owned statements using regularly more than 30 GB of HANA memory, please open an incident. In case of queries on CDS views defined in customer extensibility, please check if the CDS can be defined in a different way.
  4. If data footprint growth looks relevant, identify the business objects with the most significant contribution to the data footprint. Starting point should be ‘Top Tables – Table Size’ app in CALM
  5. After having identified the main business objects to look into, go to the details section and discuss the suggested actions (e.g. deletion or data archiving via Information Lifecycle Management) with the business owners. Be aware that decision making regarding data deletion or archiving may take several months and can involve a period of intensive testing, so that the business owners can check on side-effects.

SAP’s Foundational Success Plan provides the guidance, content, and empowerment you need to define a Data Volume Management (DVM) roadmap to stabilize system growth and reduce existing database size. This may be especially useful for steps 4 and 5. Available remote services can be requested via the ‘Get Support’ application in SAP for Me (SAP Note 1296527)

Options for data reduction – by application component

BC-BMT – Business Workflows

What is this data about?

Business workflows are used to manage the processing of tasks e.g. by sequencing tasks and the assignment of tasks to business users. A commonly used scenario is approval workflows in the purchasing area.

How to identify if you should look into it?

Check app Flexible Workflow Administration to get an overview on the existing workflow instances. Alternatively, you can use app Workflow Administration to see workflows that are currently in an active status. This view will not provide an insight into the total data volume of all existing workflows as it excludes completed workflows. Still, it may be useful to get an idea on the daily processed data.

Options on data reduction

Workflows can be archived with ILM object WORKITEM. Only fully processed workflows in a completed state can be archived.

How can data be accessed after being archived?

Use app Archive Explorer.

BC-DB – Table change protocols

What is this data about?

When defining a table, a decision is made whether all changes of data within this table should be logged and written to a table change protocol. Usually, this logging is only activated in exceptional cases, e.g. to keep track of changes on configuration tables. Using this feature for tables containing transactional data is NOT recommended.

For SAP-owned tables, SAP is responsible for the management of the data created. By default, data is kept forever and only removed in case table logging was switched on by mistake.

By using the extensibility feature of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP customers and partners can define tables via ABAP Development Tool (ADT) and decide about table logging and activate it.

  1. Open ADT (ABAP Development Tools) and navigate to your custom table
  2. Expand the table node in the Project Explorer to access technical settings
  3. Locate the "Log Changes" option in the technical table settings

Unlike change documents, simple table logging requires no additional RAP implementation. The logging is automatically handled once the flag is activated. This approach provides a straightforward method for auditing changes to custom tables in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition without requiring complex development work.

Display of change logs:

Business Configuration Change Logs can be viewed in the context of a SSCUI (Business Configuration -> Manage Your Solution -> Configure Your Solution’ or an IMG Activity (app Implementation Activities). Alternately,

  1. Change logs for the changes made in 'Current Settings' objects (those that are editable in all systems including 'Production') can be viewed via the Business Configuration Local Settings Change Logs app. (Job Template Name: STAB_BC_LOCAL_SETTINGS_LOGS_JTN)
  2. Change logs can be downloaded via the Application Job Generic UI app - 'Business Configuration Change Logs Overview' app (Job Template Name: STAB_BC_LOG_LIST_JTN)

For configurations created by SAP customers or partners, the change logs can be accessed in the Custom Business Configurations app.

How to identify if you should look into it?

As there is no overview of table change logs you may first check if table change logging is active for your own defined tables and if possible deactivate it OR request a Data Volume Management (DVM) service to support with further analysis, but be aware that the ILM destruction actions you can take will only effect the logs on your own defined tables.

Options on data reduction

Avoidance: If you identify that logging of changes is activated on non-business configuration related tables (e.g. transactional data), deactivate the flag.
ILM destruction: For existing table change logs, you can use the ILM destruction object BC_DBLOGS_DESTRUCTION_C.

BC-MID (Middleware) - IDocs

What is this data about?

IDocs can be considered as a data container for asynchronous data exchange via communication scenarios. (see help)

How to identify if you should look into it?

As IDoc data is not covered in ILM Advisor, the only indication is the BC-MID component showing relevant size. Check your activated communication scenarios. Maybe some of them use ‘IDOC interface’ as an application protocol as in this example:

Also check message monitoring (e.g. app Overview Message Monitoring or Message Monitoring for Administrators or Message Monitoring for Integration Experts) to see if data in some interfaces has piled up and is stuck in an error status. Interfaces will only become visible if there is an assignment between recipients and users. See app Assign recipients to Users and SAP Note 3358732 - AIF - How to assign AIF recipients to users?

Options on data reduction

ILM archiving or destruction: Use Information Lifecycle Management (ILM) with object IDOC to either archive the data or schedule an immediate destruction. Be aware that archiving or destruction will only work for IDocs in a final processing status. IDocs in error status are excluded. In case you have found a large number of IDocs in an error status, get in touch with SAP (e.g.  your assigned SAP contact) to find a solution.

How can data be accessed after being archived?

As IDoc data is stored in a clustered form on the HANA database, the Archive Explorer app to display archived data will only display the clustered data, which is hard to interpret.

BC-SEC - Security Audit Log

What is this data about?

The system keeps automatically track of potentially security relevant events, e.g. user logon or password check has failed. It can be accessed via the Display Security Audit Log app.

How to identify if you should look into it?

The related ILM object BC_SAL is displayed in ILM Advisor. The size figure may be bigger than the actual required memory (as explained above)

Options on data reduction

Avoidance: As often a significant number of logs is related to logon activities – especially in systems with high volume API interfaces which cause constant and highly repetitive logon activities, it may be an option to check the API configuration and see if larger packages can be processed causing fewer individual logons.

Archiving or ILM destruction: Security Audit Logs can be archived or destroyed by ILM object BC_SAL. There is a hardcoded minimum residence time of 201 days prior to archiving and a retention time of 720 days prior destruction defined.

Some of the security audit logs are managed by SAP and required for secure cloud operation processes. This subset of security audit logs CANNOT be archived or destroyed by you.

How can data be accessed after being archived?

The app Display Security Audit Log offers a selection by ‘Log Datasource’.

BC-SRV - Basis Services

In many cases, the data assigned to application component BC-SRV is of significant data volume:

  • Change documents
  • Application logs and
  • AIF (Application Interface Framework) data

All 3 objects are covered by ILM Advisor and although the size on disk is displayed, you will still get an idea where the big portion of the BC-SRV data may come from and can take a decision whether first to focus on change documents or on AIF data.

BC-SRV – Change documents

What is this data about?

All changes on business objects are tracked. Depending on the application, not only changes but also insert and delete activities are recorded. Change documents are strongly bundled with the business object.

Change documents are usually displayed in the context of the related business object. Example: Maintain Business Parter app:

Result:

In addition to change documents created within SAP defined business processes, there is also the option to activate change documents on customer- or partner-defined objects. This is possible in the apps Custom Business Objects and Custom Business Objects (New Version). Please also see the documentation on the creation of customer business objects here.

Note: If your custom business object is used for storing large amounts of data that changes frequently, the change document feature might have a negative impact on system performance and cause significant growth.

Regardless of the amount of data, change documents need to be archived regularly using the Information Lifecycle Management (ILM) apps. Otherwise, system performance can be impacted. The generic archiving object CHANGEDOCU can be used to archive change documents independent of the related business object. You can find the change document object of your custom business object on the General Information tab in the Custom Business Objects app. See the user assistance on Information Lifecycle Management for details on the archiving process.

Be aware that change documents related to custom business objects, are not displayed in app Display Change Documents (see SAP Note 3552721).

How to identify if you should look into it?

The Display Change Documents app does not provide an easy to digest overview. If there is too much data selected, the app might also not respond. Furthermore, some changes are recorded only during upgrades and therefore do not happen on a daily basis, so they may not be easy to see if the search period does not exactly match the timeframe they were created.

The ILM Advisor app partially shows the footprint of change documents together with the related business object, e.g. business partner. Keep in mind that the size figure may be bigger than the actual required memory (as explained above).

These are the most commonly growing change documents. The change document object name can be used in app Display Change Documents for selection.

Change document object

Description

Comment

PFCG

Role Maintenance

See screenshots below

SUSR_PROF

Profiles and Authorizations

See screenshots below

CATS_DATA

Time Sheets

For every time sheet entry created in e.g. app ‘My Timesheet’ 2 change documents are created, one for insert and one for an additional update.

BELEG

Accounting Document

Usually archived with related business object with archiving object FI_DOCUMNT.

MATERIAL

Material Master

As master data is hardly archived, use archiving object CHANGEDOCU

APOC_OR_CD

Output Requests

Related to transactional data, e.g. purchase orders, sales orders, billing documents. Will be archived automatically when the related business object is archived.

COND_A

Pricing Conditions

As master data is rarely archived, use archiving object CHANGEDOCU

If none of the listed change document objects match your case, ask your SAP contact person for a Data Volume Management (DVM) service for a deep-dive analysis.

The following screenshots show examples how to display change documents in the business apps.

Change document object PFCG:- app Maintain Business Roles:

With ‘Display changes’ you can navigate to the individual changes:

Change document object SUSR_PROF: app Maintain Business User:

Overview of changes:

Options on data reduction

  • Avoidance: Switch off the creation of change documents on custom business objects. (see screenshot above). This will keep existing change documents but will stop creation of new change documents.
  • Archiving: Use the archiving object of the corresponding business object (for transactional data, e.g. sales order) or use archiving object CHANGEDOCU to archive the change documents independent of the related business object (i.e. for master data, e.g. business partners, suppliers, customers, material).

How can data be accessed after being archived?

Use app Archive Explorer

BC-SRV – Application Interface Framework

What is this data about?

AIF (Application Interface Framework) enables you to monitor inbound and outbound messages from and to your SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition tenant. Data growth in the AIF application can be either caused by the administrative data of the messages transferred and by the content of the message itself. AIF supports the transfer of data via various container types, e.g. IDocs (see section IDocs above) or XML containers. So, depending on the setup, mainly the meta-data of the messages (incl. application logs and status updates) causes the data growth or the payload of the message itself stored in an XML container also grows.

How to identify if you should look into it?

Check message monitoring (e.g. app Overview Message Monitoring or Message Monitoring for Administrators or Message Monitoring for Integration Experts) to see if data in some interfaces has piled up and is stuck in an error status. Be aware that interfaces will only become visible if there is an assignment between recipients and users. See app Assign recipients to Users

3358732 - AIF - How to assign AIF recipients to users? - SAP for Me

Options on data reduction

Avoidance: Make sure to only receive and send messages at the intended frequency. Experience shows that sometimes interface processes got activated by mistake and remain active unnoticed, or data is exchanged too often.

Deletion: An automatic housekeeping is executed regularly and deletes messages for which deletion is allowed by configuration. Check if deletion is activated for your most important namespaces / interfaces, if there is no need for data archiving as there is no need to keep the data for a longer timeframe.

Navigation: Business Configuration: Application Area: ‘Application Platform and Infrastructure’ -> Sub Application Area: ‘Process Management and Integration’ -> Message Monitoring -> ‘Configure Interface Version - Deletion Settings’.

Alternatively, you use the ‘Implementation Activities’ app: General Settings -> Further Settings -> Configure Interface Version - Deletion Settings.

In case there is already a default number of days proposed by SAP, this can be overwritten.

Example:

Note: only messages in a completed state will be deleted. In case you face a high number of messages in an error status, please see SAP Note 3434004 for a mass cancellation of error messages. This step will transfer the messages in a ‘deletable’ status.

Archiving or ILM destruction: You can configure ILM object AIF_MES (which relates to archiving object /AIF/MES) and run either direct destruction or data archiving for AIF messages.

Note: as the AIF interface also uses application log during processing inbound or outbound service messages, deletion or archiving of the messages will also remove the related application log entries.

How can data be accessed after being archived?

In general, archived messages can be accessed via app Archive Explorer, but keep in mind that the payload of the messages, in case it was in a binary format, will not be displayed in a readable format.

BC-SRV – Application Logs

What is this data about?

Many actions performed in a business process will create application logs to allow you to keep track of what has happened, e.g. to access the result of an executed job, e.g. dunning run or automatic clearing run.

How to identify if you should look into it?

As there is no app to display application logs from all possible applications, it is difficult to determine which application, or job causes most logs. Application logs are displayed in apps that are specific to the respective application and can only be accessed by users with the respective business role assigned. When searching for ‘application log’ in SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library you will get an impression on how many business processes create application logs.

In case you want to get insights into the dominating application that creates application log volume, request a Data Volume Management (DVM) service through your SAP contact.

Options on data reduction

Avoidance: Some processes cause many error logs when not configured correctly. Adjusting the process will help to reduce the number of created logs.

Deletion: Application logs with an expiration date in the past are deleted automatically. You can check the expiration date by adding the respective column to your app.

List view including expiration date:

Experience shows that in some systems there are many application logs created by revenue recognition processes. See SAP Note 3403377 for the pre-defined expiration dates. There is an option to adjust the expiration dates on request for your individual tenant.

Archiving: There is no data archiving option available. Archiving object BC_SBAL is not active in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Some applications archive or delete the related application logs when the business objects are archived. One example is the Application Interface Framework (AIF), so deleting or archiving AIF messages can also affect the application log data footprint.

CA-CPD – Commercial Project Management

What is this data about?

This is the data of Professional Service Projects; often it is planning data that contributes to the data growth.

How to identify if you should look into it?

Check the number of projects and the lifetime of your projects and the number of plan data per project. This gives the first indication.

Options on data reduction

Avoidance: Make sure that the Project has a relevant end date. End dates that are very far in the future will create more plan data.

Archiving: Use archiving object CPD_SS (engagement project)

How can data be accessed after being archived?

Besides Archive Explorer, there is currently (release 2508) no business user facing app to display archived project data in a business-like and meaningful way.

CA-MDG – Master Data Governance – Data Replication Framework

What is this data about?

The data replication framework is used to exchange data with connected systems. Example: upload of G/L Accounts from SAP S/4HANA to Ariba.

How to identify if you should look into it?

There are two kinds of data that are growing: Replication Status und Replication Logs.

For Replication Logs check app ‘Monitor Replication’.

View on replication logs:

For the replication status check app ‘Display Replication Status’:

This app will only allow you to check the status of a selected business object. You do not get an overview of the business object types, that caused most of the replication status data. So, you can either derive the most relevant interfaces from the ‘Monitor Replication’ app or request a Data Volume Management (DVM) service for an in-depth analysis (see SAP Note 1296527.)

Options on data reduction

Deletion: There are two regularly scheduled housekeeping jobs: SAP_MD_DRF_SERVOUT_LOG_DEL to delete replication logs
and
SAP_MD_DRF_MDGD_CP_DEL to delete change pointers of master data governance.
The default retention time is set to 90 days. You can check or adjust the default in the business configuration in section: Database and Data Management -> DRF Replication -> Configuration for Data Replication Framework > Specify Days after which the Data from DRF is deleted.

Archiving or ILM destruction: Use ILM object DRF_REPSTA to use the ILM destruction option to delete or to archive the replication status data. In many cases, ILM destruction is the recommended option unless you see a necessity to keep this data long term.

How can data be accessed after being archived?

Use Archive Explorer app for display. There is no view of archived data integrated into the Replication Framework apps.

CO-PC – Product Cost Controlling

What is this data about?

Most data in product cost controlling is usually caused by actual costing document information incl. the cost component split data. This data is used in the Management Accounting and Margin Analysis applications.

How to identify if you should look into it?

In case of many goods movements, it is highly likely that correspondingly the number of actual costing documents is significant. You can get an idea on the volume by running an archiving run for material ledger documents for a single period in test mode and check the archive logs for the number of records.
Note: prior to execute archiving the ILM configuration of archiving object FCML4H_DOC is required.

Options on data reduction

Archiving: You can use archiving object FCML4H_DOC. You can archive data up to the period before last. This includes

  • Quantity and value movement data for materials
  • Aggregated data of value movements per material and period
  • Quantity and value movement data with cost component split
  • Aggregated data of value movement data with cost component split

How can data be accessed after being archived?

Use app Archive Explorer to access individual records. Reporting on totals records is still intact after archiving. Archived data are aggregated and will be posted as beginning inventory in the period AFTER the last archived period. Example: after having archived all data up to period 12/2024, then the aggregated values per material will be posted as beginning inventory in period 01/2025. Therefore, inconsistencies in the evaluation are avoided.

FI-GL – General Leger Accounting

What is this data about?

Growth in this application component is driven by accounting document line items and universal journal items.

How to identify if you should look into it?

Check app ILM Advisor for size of ILM object FI_DOCUMNT.

Options on data reduction

Archiving: Use ILM object FI_DOCUMNT. Be aware that there is a hardcoded residence time which prevents archiving of accounting documents created or edited in the last 2 years.

How can data be accessed after being archived?

The individual accounting documents or line items can be displayed in apps

  • Display Document, Display Journal Entries
  • Display G/L Accounts Line Items - For Ledger
  • …

As the balance forward criteria are preserved by data archiving, i.e. the ‘totals’ records are kept, reporting on a totals level, e.g. by period is not affected. E.g the Trial Balance app will return correct results. The main limitation in reporting is that the drill-down to line-item details will no longer work after having archived the accounting documents.

FI-LOC – Statutory Reports

What is this data about?

The component FI-LOC (localization) covers several tables. Usually creating statutory reports contributes most to data growth. reports.

How to identify if you should look into it?

Check the number of reports and tasks in app Run Statutory Reports. This is

Options on data reduction

Deletion of an individual run: Use the ‘Delete’ run option in the ‘Run statutory reports’ app

Mass Deletion: Use option 'Clean up reporting tasks' (see Fiori App Library or User Assistance) in the business configuration. Finance -> Statutory Reporting -> Basic Settings -> Clean Up Reporting Tasks:

Select 'Delete Accepted Tasks', if you want the program to delete the reporting tasks with status 'Completed' or 'Content Accepted by the Government'. Note: The 'Delete Accepted Tasks' option is disabled in the production environment.

Select 'Force Delete', if you want the program to anyway delete the selected reporting tasks. This option allows to ignore errors, if any, in the selected reporting tasks.

Although the clean-up is accessed via the business configuration, it is NOT a setting for an automatically regularly executed deletion report. Instead, it has to be re-triggered manually from time to time (e.g. every month or quarter).

Data archiving: Use archiving object SRF_TASK to move no longer required tasks from the database to archive files.

How can data be accessed after being archived?

Use app Archive Explorer.

FIN-CS – Financial Consolidation

In the FIN-CS (Financial Consolidation) area, there are two sets of data that show growth: Task Logs for Financial Consolidation and Intercompany Matching Journals for Group Reporting.

FIN-CS – Financial Consolidation Task Logs

What is this data about?

Group Reporting allows you to consolidate financial data from various sources. The consolidation process consists of several consolidation tasks. The logs of the tasks are accessible in app ‘Task Logs’.

Entry screen:

Drilldown to log details:

How to identify if you should look into it?

Check app ‘Task Logs’ (see above) or app ‘Consolidation Monitor’.

Options on data reduction

Archiving: Use archiving object FINCS_LOG (Task Logs). You can use the condition fields: dimension, ledger, log date or version to define specific residence and retention times. In the archive write job variant you can select the data to be archived by ledger, version and log date.

How can data be accessed after being archived?

Use app Archive Explorer.

FIN-CS – Intercompany Matching Journals

What is this data about?

Intercompany Matching and Reconciliation (ICMR) is introduced to speed up your intercompany reconciliation process from company close to corporate close. As a built-in solution in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, it matches transactions without any ETL (extract, transform, load) processes and reconciles your financial data in real time.

In a matching run, the system rolls in from the data source the data that meets the filter conditions set in the matching method and data source, matches the data based on your input period and the matching rules, and saves the items and results as ’Matching Journal Entries’. The matched items are grouped into assignments, while the unmatched items are also tagged with the unmatched status.

How to identify if you should look into it?

Check app ‘Manage Matching Documents’ that also shows matched documents marked as deleted. Those matching documents marked as deleted are still in the database. It is suggested that these documents should be deleted physically by using ILM destruction object ICA_JOURNAL_DES. Alternatively, you can check the data in app ‘Display Matching Items’.

Options on data reduction

Deletion: Use ILM object ICA_JOURNAL_DES for data destruction.

Archiving: Use ILM object ICA_JOURNA for data archiving.

How can data be accessed after being archived?

Use app Archive Explorer.

FIN-FSCM-CR – Credit Management

What is this data about?

Often the ‘limit formula step results’ in credit management are the main drivers of data growth in the FIN-FSCM component. There is the option to activate events, e.g. when new information e.g. provided by credit rating agencies is imported; certain values change at a business partner, so that the score and credit limit get automatically re-calculated. These events can trigger a re-calculation of score and credit limit. Depending on the number of business partners in credit management, this can cause a huge growth in the number of step results. Also scheduling the jobs to update the score and credit limit in a high frequency can be the cause of huge data growth.

How to identify if you should look into it?

Use app ‘Manage Credit Accounts’, select a business partner and check for the ‘Score Evaluation Log’.

Drill-down to details to see the scoring evaluation result and the scoring evaluation steps.

Options on data reduction

Deletion: Schedule delete jobs via app ‘Delete Credit Management Data’. Probably the most relevant job should be SAP_FIN_UKM_FORMULA_DELETE_LOGS (Delete Logs for Calculating Scoring and Credit Limits), but you should also revise the scheduling frequency of other jobs mentioned in SAP Note 3548031.

The screenshot shows the parameters for job template SAP_FIN_UKM_FORMULA_DELETE_LOGS. It is recommended to run the job twice: for credit limit logs and for scoring logs.

LE-SHP – Shipping - Collective Processing Log

What is this data about?

Collective processing logs are related to activities in the Quote to Cash business process. These logs track errors in

  • Collective Delivery Processing: Logs errors encountered during mass delivery creation or updates, enabling efficient error resolution
  • Batch Billing Operations: Captures billing-related errors during collective invoice processing to ensure accurate financial postings
  • Mass Sales Document Updates: Records issues during bulk modifications to sales orders, quotations, or contracts

How to identify if you should look into it?

Use ‘My Collective Run Logs’ app to check on existing logs

Options on data reduction

Use ILM destruction object LE_SHP_LOG_DESTRUCTION

MM-IM – Inventory Management - Goods Movements

What is this data about?

Goods receipts and goods issues are tracked as goods movements.

How to identify if you should look into it?

Check app ILM Advisor to see if ILM object MM_MATBEL appears with a significant size. Also, app ‘Goods Movement Analysis’ can provide valuable insights.

Options on data reduction

Data archiving with ILM object MM_MATBEL.

How can data be accessed after being archived?

Use app Archive Explorer.

Good to know: the following stock identifying fields per period are preserved even after archiving, so that this aggregated information per period still exists after archiving and can be used by apps:

  • Batch Number (Stock Identifier)
  • Addl Supplier SID
  • Customer for Special Stock
  • Special Stock Valuation
  • Stock Type
  • Storage Location (Stock Identifier)
  • Supplier for Special Stock
  • Sales Order Number of Valuated Sales Order Stock
  • Sales Order Item of Valuated Sales Order Stock
  • Valuated Sales Order Stock WBS Element
  • Material in Respect of Which Stock is Managed
  • Base Unit of Measure
  • Special Stock Indicator
  • Currency Key
  • Plant
  • Vendor Stock Valuation Indicator
  • Cost Estimate Number - Product Costing
  • Parallel Unit of Measure (Stock Identifier)
  • Period/Year

SD-BF – Pricing conditions

What is this data about?

Pricing conditions can be linked to pricing relevant line items in either the sales or purchasing process. Most commonly, most pricing conditions are related to sales order items or billing document items. Pricing conditions also often exist on purchase order items, but usually they don’t contribute to data volumes as much as the sales-related pricing conditions. Depending on the complexity of the pricing model, the number of conditions per line item varies significantly. Complex pricing models can cause up to 80 pricing records per e.g. sales order line item.

How to identify if you should look into it?

Check app ILM Advisor if ILM objects SD_VBAK (Sales Orders), SD_VBRK (Billing Documents) or MM_EKKO (Purchase Orders) are of significant size. As pricing conditions are archived together with the related business object, the size of the pricing conditions is added to the leading business object.

Besides checking ILM Advisor, you may also talk to the pricing experts in your sales department and ask them to check some example business objects (e.g. sales orders or billing document) for the number of pricing conditions related to them.

Options on data reduction

Archiving: There is no dedicated ILM archiving object for pricing conditions. Instead, pricing conditions are archived with the related business objects. The business objects most commonly tributing most to pricing condition growth are SD_VBAK (Sales Orders) and SD_VBRK (Billing Documents).

How can data be accessed after being archived?

  • Billing Documents: App ‘Display Billing Documents from Archive’
  • Sales Orders: App ‘Display Sales Orders - VA03’
  • Purchase Orders: App ‘Display Purchase Order – Advanced’

entry.title: PR Re-approval after deleted PR Line
entry.content:
Dear All,

In flexible workflow, In the case where a Purchase Requisition (PR) has already been approved, is it possible to cancel it by deleting the PR item and have the system trigger the approval workflow again?

Regards,

Keratiya D.

entry.title: SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 2: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 and SAP Fiori Launchpad
entry.content:
This second post in my series gives an overview of user experience innovations that become generally available with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 release. Highlights are further AI-assisted features: for personalizing My Home, getting explanations for error messages, and Situation Handling recommendations. We also provide the often-requested capability to launch apps via transaction code or SAP Fiori ID, the new “required reading” feature for news in My Home, and mobile phone optimization – to name a few.

Users in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition can use Joule to access their data: via web browser on desktop, or mobile, via SAP Mobile Start, which also provides access to applications directly. Read about innovations for Joule and SAP Mobile Start in the first blog post introducing the series, and providing information about SAP Build Work Zone:

  • SAP UX Q1/2026 Update – Part 1: AI, Joule, SAP Build Work Zone, SAP Mobile Start.

Below, you will get an overview of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition UX innovations in these areas:

SAP Fiori launchpad
  o.  New shell bar now the default
  o.  Launch apps directly via Transaction Code / Fiori-ID
  o.  Making New Apps Available to Users
      -  Activating new apps, since now delivered inactive
      -  New Experience Switch
   o.  Further improvements for the launchpad
My Home
  o.  Manage News app now with “must be read” feature
  o.  Enhanced Personalization
  o.  AI-Assisted UX Features:
       -  AI-Assisted Smart Personalization of My Home for Applications
       -  AI-Assisted Error Explanation
       -  AI-Assisted Situation Handling
SAP Fiori elements mobile phone optimization
UI Adaptation
Improvements in details

Beyond these, we have a number of interesting new UX innovations for beta testing and early adopters with the 2602 release. I plan to cover these in my next blog post in the next few days.

Many of the UX improvements in details are enabled by our UI framework (SAPUI5 and SAP Fiori elements) and hence also available to customers and partners developing their own applications. Note however that the enabled AI-assisted features are only available for extensions built directly on the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system.

Below you’ll see AI innovations from our UX framework as well as some selected application highlights. You can get a more comprehensive overview of the latest AI innovations in these blog posts, which also include application specific examples:

  • Highlights of the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 release.
  • AI innovations highlights in SAP Cloud ERP 2602.
  • Artificial Intelligence in SAP Cloud ERP 2602.

 

SAP Fiori Launchpad

Let’s look closer at these UX updates:

  • New shell bar now the default, with updated design of notifications and user menu.
  • Launch apps directly via Transaction Code / Fiori-ID.
  • Further improvements.

New shell bar now the default

We have made design improvements which are relevant for a consistent user experience across the entire SAP Business Suite. The new shell bar first became available as an opt-in choice for customers with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2508 as described in my blog post covering 2508. With the 2602 release, it is now activated by default.

Benefits of the New Shell Bar

The new shell bar has these benefits:

  • The product name is displayed prominently now to the right of the SAP logo. In this case the name is “S/4HANA Cloud”.
  • Updated notifications design.
  • Updated user menu design.

Product Name Displayed

The immediately visible difference with the new shell bar is that the product name is displayed to the right of the SAP Logo, in this case “S/4HANA Cloud” as you can see at the top of Figure 1. The logo and the name are all contained within a clickable area, which brings the user to the start page (i.e. My Home).

Figure 1: New shell bar, now activated by default. Alt Text: A screenshot of Manage Sales Orders app, with the shell header highlighted, looking as described in the text.

Updated User Menu

The new user menu design shown in Figure 2 provides more space for the avatar of the user, and provides more space between items for easier selection. The settings are always at the top as the first entry, and the “Sign Out” option is separated from the actual navigation options in the list, to make it easier to find.

 

Figure 2: Updated design of user menu and notifications. Alt Text: On the left an image of the previous user menu and the updated user menu. On the right an image of the updated notifications list, showing four notifications in the group “Last Month”, each with a title, two lines of description and a line with the data and a “more” link.

Updated Notifications Design

The new simplified design of notifications shown on the right in Figure 2  fits better with the overall design of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, and provides simplier interactions. By default notifications are sorted by date, with grouping by “Today”, “Yesterday”, “Last Week”, “Last Month”, as you can see in Figure 3 below. Users can also choose to sort by importance.

 Figure 3: Updated design of notifications. Alt Text: A short video showing the user selecting to display the list of notifications in the groups “Today”, “Yesterday” and “This Week”. 

Recommendation: Check Test Automation and RPA

We advise that you check any test automation and RPA (Robotic Process Automation) you may have, since you may need to make some adjustments – in particular if you access the user menu, for example to log off at the end of a test.

In case you need more time to do this, you can opt-out of the new shell bar for this release.You can switch it off via “Activate New Features”, Feature “/UI2/SHELL_BAR_TOGGLE”.

Important: do switch it back on before next upgrade! There is no opt-out planned from 2608 onwards.

Launch apps directly via Transaction Code

Customers have been requesting this for quite a while now, so I am happy to announce that with the 2602 release, users can launch applications directly via the transaction code (t-code).

This is particularly helpful for those users who have experience with older SAP ERP systems, such as SAP ECC (ERP Central Component), and are used to launching transactions by entering the “t-code” (transaction code) in the SAP GUI for Windows shell bar. To launch a transaction VA01 (Create sales order) in SAP GUI for Windows, users enter “/nVA01” if they want it to open in the same window, and “/oVA01” if they want it to open in a new window. Lower case “/nva01” or “/ova01” also works. Furthermore, users could go to the start page by entering “/n” or “/o”, opening it either in the same window or in a new window respectively.

We have implemented the same logic in the search field in the launchpad shell bar, but with “/o” now creating a new browser tab rather than opening a new window.

We have also extended it, so that users can also enter the t-code after “/n” or “/o”. Figure 4 shows what it looks like, and Figure 5 shows a short video of it in action for an example using /nmm03 (Display Material) to open the classic UI and /nF217A (Customer – 360 View) to open a Fiori app.

 

Figure 4: Launching a classic UI via the transaction code “va01”. Alt Text: The shell header is shown, with the search field showing “All” and the search term /nva01 entered.

 

Figure 5: Launching applications directly via transaction code. Alt Text: A short video showing the user entering /nmm03 and /nF217A as described in the text.

You can determine an application’s t-code via the About entry in the launchpad user menu. We have also assigned transaction codes to Fiori apps, they are often identical to the Fiori ID, but not always.

Read more about it in this blog post:

  • Navigate SAP Fiori launchpad with Ease: Launch Apps via Transaction Codes.

Making New Apps Available to Users

New apps for business users can help them work more effectively, and get their work done more easily – but only if they actually use them! That means that they need to be activated and hence made available to users in the first place.

When one or more new applications are delivered that replace an existing application, users of the existing application need to be informed that a replacement is available to them. This is handled by the New Experience Switch. This is especially important if the existing application is about to be deprecated.

The next two sections cover these two topics in more detail: activating new apps, and the New Experience Switch.

Activating new apps, since now delivered inactive

With the 2602 release we no longer activate new apps automatically, so as not to disrupt existing users, as well as to ensure that users are not automatically granted authorizations that they should not have.

In order to activate an application, i.e. a Fiori app or a classic UI, the underlying IAM  (Identity and Application Management) apps of type “App Authorization Variant” need to be activated. These particular IAM apps were introduced a while ago in order to allow finer-grained authorizations for applications.

For example, the My Inbox app has a standard view and an expert view. You will only be able to access the expert view if the corresponding IAM app for the expert view has been activated by your administrator. If your administrator has only activated the IAM app for the standard view, then you will be able to access the My Inbox app, but only its standard view.

Since new apps provided by SAP are no longer activated automatically, administrators need to activate the underlying IAM apps of type “App Authorization Variant”. Only then will users be able to find the apps via the launchpad search or in the App Finder, see the corresponding tiles on pages in the launchpad, and assign them as favorites in My Home.

Find out more in the documentation:

  • Work with IAM Apps (App Authorization Variants)
  • How to Activate or Deactivate IAM Apps (App Authorization Variants)

New Experience Switch

The New Experience Switch informs users that a replacement of the application that they have opened is available to them, and offers a toggle in the shell bar to directly switch to the new application. You can see what this looks like in the top left image in Figure 6.

If the existing, old version of the app is planned to be deprecated and removed in a future release beyond the next one, then the information icon in the shell bar next to the New Version toggle is replaced by a warning icon, as shown in the top right image in Figure 6.

Finally, if the existing, old version will be removed with the upgrade to the next release, an alert is shown instead of a warning, as shown in the bottom image in Figure 6.

Figure 6: New Experience Switch. Clockwise from top left: Information, Warning and Alert pop-overs. Alt Text: Three screenshots showing the respective popovers, each with a button “Switch to New Version”. The first two include a carousel giving information about the benefits of the new version. The last one include in red “August 2025” as the month when the app is planned to be removed.

The three phases described above were introduced already with the 2508 release.

With the 2602 release, we also support cases where one application has multiple successors. Figure 7 shows an example.

Figure 7: New Experience Switch warning for an application with multiple successors. Alt Text: Screenshot showing the popover containing two cards, one for each of the specialized versions available as successors.

Also new with 2602 is the option for users to provide feedback when the open up the new version of an application (Figure 8). When they select this, a survey is opened with one page of questions about the new version of the app.

Figure 8: New Experience Switch welcome pop-over for new app, now with “Provide Feedback” button. Alt Text: Screenshot showing the pop-over, with a carousel giving information about the benefits of the new version.

To find out more, have a look at the documentation, or at the SAP note for the list of apps that currently have successors making use of the New Experience Switch:

  • Documentation: Switching to a New App Version
  • SAP Note 3545277 - New Experience Switch in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: Overview of Apps

Further Improvements for the Launchpad

Enterprise Search Shows All Smart Business Tiles

Business users can now find all their Smart Business tiles (typically insight tiles with KPIs and/or mini charts) using enterprise search in the launchpad shellbar. Since users can define their own tiles via Smart Business, with specific filter values, this means that they can also find these personal tiles using search.

Did you know that enterprise search supports a number of search operators that you can use when searching for apps, or indeed when searching for business objects? Operators such as OR, *, - and “”.

You can also further filter search results, export them, share them or save the search as a tile. Find out more in the documentation:

  • Searching and Launching Apps or Business Objects.

Administrators can Upload and Download Spaces

In the Manage Launchpad Spaces app of the SAP Fiori launchpad, administrators can download existing spaces together with their assigned pages and upload them again to another system.

This enables you to transfer spaces and their assigned pages to other systems, for example, when you can't use transports, meaning that you can improve productivity and consistency across systems. Find out more:

  • Documentation: Downloading and Uploading Spaces

 

My Home

We continue to enhance My Home to make it even more popular with users. In this release, we have:

  • Manage News app now with “must be read” feature
  • Enhanced Personalization
  • AI-assisted smart personalization of My Home for applications
    (this is covered in the next section AI-Assisted UX Innovations)

We are also providing the feature AI-Assisted Smart Solution for Situations in My Home for beta-testing, which I will showcase in part 3 in this series of blog posts.

Manage News App now with “Must be Read” Feature

News articles containing essential company information can now be designated as required reading. All users must acknowledge that they have read and understood the content.

The main benefits of this feature are:

  • Strengthens accountability: When reading is mandatory, employees can be held accountable for understanding essential information. This practice helps ensure compliance with legal, safety, and policy-related requirements.
  • Ensures consistent knowledge across the organization: All employees receive the same information, minimizing knowledge gaps and miscommunication.
  • Enhances change management : During periods of transition—such as the introduction of new policies or process changes—required reading ensures that all employees understand the reasons behind and the impacts of those changes.

Figure 9 shows what this looks like for a user: a required reading pop-over appears on their screen. The article has also been flagged as “Containing Critical News”.

Figure 9: An example of a news article that is required reading. Alt Text: A screenshot of My Home with a pop-over “Required Reading” that is also highlighted with “Contains Critical News”. The news article has an image at the top, followed by text showing the first part of the article. The “Done” button is greyed out, the “Remind me later” button is visible.

Figure 10 below shows the Manage News app, with the toggles Critical and the new Required Reading one.

Figure 10: The Manage News app has a toggle to mark articles as “Required Reading”. Alt Text: Screenshot of the Manage News app with the toggles set to “Yes”, and some news text in the Description field.

Enhanced Personalization

A great strength of My Home is the degree to which users can personalize it to suit their needs. We have now enhanced personalization further by letting users control the visibility of News and Pages separately within the News and Pages section of My Home.

If they are not so interested in news, they can choose to hide it, with the additional benefit of having all the pages only take up one row of vertical space rather than two, as shown in Figure 11. Figure 12shows how users can do the personalization.

Note that even with the news hidden, they will still receive required reading: when they log on, the required reading appears as a pop-over that can only be closed by confirming that one has read and understood the content.

Alternatively, users can decide to only show news and not pages. They can of course also decide to show both news and pages, as before, or hide both.

Figure 11: My Home with enhanced personalization, showing News deselected and Pages selected. Alt Text: A screenshot of My Home, with eight cards in one row in the Pages section, below the ToDos section showing one situation and the Apps section.

Figure 12: My Home Settings pop-over with the new selectors for News and Pages within the News and Pages section. Alt Text: A screenshot showing the Layout part of My Home Settings, where users can select or deselect sections. For the News and Pages section, News is deselected, Pages are selected.

 

AI-Assisted User Experience

When AI is embedded directly into the application UI to give users a better user experience, as opposed to being accessed via the Joule window, we talk about “AI-Assisted User Experience”.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2508, we distinguish between AI base features and premium features:

  • Base features are free of charge, and only require that the customer signs the SAP AI Terms, which can be done via this link.
  • Premium features require SAP AI Units to be in place. Those listed here also require Joule Premium for Financial Management. Customers can activate this via SAP for Me -> Portfolio & Products -> Business AI.

The following are now generally available as premium features (they were both in beta testing with the 2508 release) and are described in more detail below:

  • AI-assisted smart personalization of My Home for applications.
  • AI-assisted error explanation.

This feature is still in beta, but planned to become generally available with the 2602 release on March 12th:

  • AI-assisted situation handling.
    See below for more details.

AI-Assisted Smart Personalization of My Home for Applications

You could already use natural language and AI to personalize your My Home by adding Insights Cards, since the 2508 release. Now, with 2602, you can use natural language to find and add applications and insights tiles.

To do this, use the AI button “Add Content” in the salutation bar at the top of My Home, as shown at the top of Figure X. Clicking on this brings a pop-over with the option to add Insights Cards or, as shown, Apps & Insights Tiles. By describing what you want to do, the system will propose suitable applications and Insights Tiles. If you select them and click “Add”, they will be added, either as an entry in your Apps Favorites section, or in the Insights Tiles section. Figure 13 shows these main steps.

Figure 13: AI-Assisted Smart Personalization of My Home for Applications. Alt Text: The image shows three screenshots: at the top an extract of My Home showing the blue salutation bar, with an AI button “Add Content” on the right. An arrow has been added linking it to the second screenshot, where a popover “Add Content” is shown, where the user has entered “I want to monitor sales orders what app should I use to do this”, and two of four results are listed and visualized: “Track Sales Orders” and “Manage Sales Orders” (shown as an insight tile). Below that a screenshot of the outcome in My Home: the former is added to Apps Favorites, the latter to Insights Tiles.

 Watch a demo and read more:

  • Video: AI-Assisted Smart Personalization of My Home for Applications in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (1:07 min.)
  • SAP Discovery Center: Smart Personalization of My Home.

AI-Assisted Error Explanation

New users who are unfamiliar with the system may find that the short error messages give too little information for them to fully understand the issue, and what to do about it. This is where the Summarizing and Guiding and Recommending AI-patterns can help: the AI-Assisted Error Explanation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition uses these to summarize information which explains the error and provide a recommendation for how to resolve it. Figure 14 shows an example where a user has entered a Sold-to-Party ID which does not exist in the system.

Note that this feature is also only available for applications based on SAP Fiori elements.

Figure 14: AI-Assisted Error Explanation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2502. Alt Text: On the left part of a data entry screen is shown, with the field “Sold-to Party containing “ABC” marked red and below that a  popover with an error message “No customer master record exists for sold-to-party ABC”, and in the text line below that the AI icon followed by “Generate Explanation”. A fat arrow leads from this text to the image on the right, which indicates what happens when you click on this text: you get a popover with an short Explanation section and a longer Resolution section with numbered actions.

Have a look:

  • Video: AI-Assisted Error Explanation (45 seconds)
  • SAP Discovery Center: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, error explanation.

AI-Assisted Situation Handling

The Intelligent Situation Handling framework helps users deal with not-so-frequent business situations, by notifying them of the situation, giving them an overview of the situation and providing contextual information to help resolve it. Ideally, the system also offers recommendations on how to go about resolving it.

Currently still in beta testing, we plan to make AI-assisted situation handling generally available with the 2602.1 release on March 12th.

Figure 15 shows what this looks like for a situation where a purchase contract is going to expire soon. The system is able to evaluate the company policy, in this case provided in the form of a decision matrix, and informs the user that a Request for Proposals is not needed for this contract, since its contract value is below the threshold.

Figure 15: AI-Assisted Situation Handling in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602, showing how the user is notified in My Home and can navigate to the situation page and there generate a recommended solution. ALT Text: The To-Do card “The purchase contract will expire soon” is shown at the top, with an arrow leading to the image below of the situation page, showing a “General Info” section and a “Recommended Solution” section, which only contains the text “You haven’t generated a solution yet. Would you like to do that now”, along with a “Generate” button with the AI icon. From there an arrow leads to the bottom image which shows the recommended solution text, along with thumbs-up and thumbs-down feedback buttons.

To see it in action, watch this:

  • Video: AI-Assisted Situation Handling in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (1:00 minutes).
  • SAP Discover Center: AI-assisted Situation Handling.

The extended framework for Situation Handling allows customers to define their own situations. It is very powerful, but we have received feedback that it can be challenging for first time customers and partners to get it up and running. Hence Angelika Salmen has published this blog post to help you get started:

  • My First Situation with the Extended Framework.

For further information about Situation Handling, have a look at the links at the end of the above blog post. 

 

SAP Fiori elements mobile phone optimization

We have made significant improvements in the overall user experience for accessing selected applications from your mobile phone, with layouts and interactions tailored specifically for smartphones.

The recommended way to access SAP Fiori web applications from your mobile phone is via SAP Mobile Start, which treats web applications the same way as native mobile applications: users can define favorites, and see all their apps, both web and native mobile, in the Apps view.

The currently supported SAP Fiori elements based applications are now rendered in a more user-friendly way, reducing complexity for users on the go by removing non-essential features, patterns and services that are not suited for mobile use.

This results in improved productivity and speed with simplified navigation, faster access to actions and optimized information density.

The new layout is provided by the mobile mode. Users can switch on the previous desktop mode in case they need access to any of the non-essential features hidden in the mobile mode. Figure 16 shows an example of a list report in the desktop mode (on the left) and what it now looks like rendered in the mobile mode (on the right).

Figure 16: SAP Fiori elements mobile phone optimization. On the left a list report rendered using the previous desktop mode, on the right using the new mobile mode. Alt Text: screenshots of a list report as it would appear on a mobile phone. On the left with filter bar and filters visible, so that only one line of the list is shown; on the right they are hidden, so that three lines of content are shown.

Currently these five apps are supported:

  • My Home
  • Customer 360 View (F2187A)
  • Manage Direct Activity Allocation (F3697A)
  • Manage Material Serial Numbers (F6879)
  • Manage Production Supply Areas (F6935)
  • Serial Number History (F7368)

We are eager to learn from our customers to see how well the current functionality meets users real-life needs, and where we could improve the experience. If you are interested in trying this out and providing feedback, please sign up for our Early Adopter Care program.

 

UI Adaptation

Adapt UI is a key user extensibility tool for making no-code / low-code, upgrade-safe adaptations and extensions to SAP Fiori apps. We have continuously enhanced its scope, so that it now supports most of the common changes.

A key user is a business expert who is authorized to make changes on behalf of other users – for example a team member who is interested in technology. The goal of key user extensibility is to empower business users to be able to adapt their working environment to suit their needs, without having to rely on system administrators.

Adapt UI is easy and safe to use: you can undo/redo changes, revert to a previously activated version or even revert to the original app.

Many Fiori apps contain object pages, that summarize information on a business object such as a sales order or a product. Adapt UI offers a large amount of options for adapting and extending object pages:

  • Hide/show SAP-defined fields
  • Add custom fields created with the SAP Fiori app Custom Fields (ID F1481)
  • Combine/split/rearrange/group/ungroup fields
  • Hide/show SAP-defined tabs, sections, tables, or action buttons
  • Change the configuration of tables (view settings, initial load, scrolling thresholds etc. – as featured in my previous blog post)
  • Change text arrangement (ID only, Text first, Text las) – also featured in my previous blog post.
  • Rename labels
  • Hide/show object header content
  • Embed web-based content in the object header or a section.

Key users who are authorized to use adapt UI can open it directly from their user menu in the launchpad. Once in started, Adapt UI shows you directly on the screen what changes you can make.

To find out more about this and watch a demo showing how it works and how easy it is, I thoroughly recommend this 13-minute video:

Video 1: Extending SAP Fiori Object Pages with Adapt UI. Click on the image to go to the video.The video shows you:

  • How to recognize an object page
  • What is Adapt UI
  • Prerequisites for Adapt UI
  • Demo
  • Tips for Working with Adapt UI
  • Key Takeaways
  • Resources worth knowing. These are:
    • SAP Design System - SAP Fiori: Object Page Floorplan
    • SAP Help - SAP Fiori Launchpad User Guide: ​Adapting SAP Fiori UIs at Runtime – Key User Adaptation.
    • SAP Learning - Tutorials: Key User Adaptation for SAPUI5 Applications.

Note that the video was created for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, so the transport step at the end is different in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition – all the rest is relevant also for public edition, which is why I included it here.

The Adapt UI functionality is powered by SAPUI5 Flexibility technology.

 

Improvements in Details

User research has shown that it is easier to work with tables that contain hundreds of entries when the tables are implemented as a grid table rather than a responsive table.

Hence, with the 2602 release, we have looked at apps containing tables that typically have hundreds of entries and have changed the type of table used from a responsive table to a grid table.

For the full list of enhancements, have a look at the What’s New Viewer:

  • UX in What’s New Viewer for  SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.

 

Continued soon with an overview of upcoming UX Innovations for Beta Testing in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602

I hope you enjoyed this second part, covering what is now generally available with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 and SAP Fiori launchpad. I will post part three in the next few days, covering exciting new UX innovations available for beta testing with the S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602 release.

Do keep posting your experiences and recommendations yourself in the SAP Community, with the SAP Fiori and/or the User Experience tag! In case you are wondering how to get a list of the most recent blog posts on SAP Fiori and User Experience in our new SAP Community, use these links:

  • Latest blog posts on SAP Fiori in the SAP Community.
  • Latest blog posts on User Experience in the SAP Community.

entry.title: Environment, Health, and Safety in SAP Cloud ERP 2602
entry.content:
Welcome to your one-stop shop to learn and share knowledge about SAP's ready-to-run public cloud ERP software solution.  

 SAP Cloud ERP is part of SAP Business Suite that brings AI, data, and applications together into a fully integrated system. This community is open for anyone in the cloud ERP ecosystem–customers, partners, and SAP experts.  

 

In this blog post I’d like to outline the innovations for SAP Cloud ERP 2602 Release in the area of Environment, Health, and Safety. These are the topics that are covered:

 

Cross Functions for Environment Management and Workplace Safety: 

  • Planning and execution of EHS inspections (7Z7)
  • Improved operational compliance overview page with new task insight metrics (4C8)

Workplace Safety:

  • Expansion of UK-specific incident regulatory reporting (3FP)
  • Investigation overview on the investigation object page (3FP)

 

In case you do not need all the information below and briefly want to get an overview of the release highlights, please watch this video with the top four key innovations:

 

 

Video#1: SAP Cloud ERP 2602 for Environment, Health, and Safety

 

 

Planning and execution of EHS inspections



Business Background
Organizations are required to conduct inspections to comply with numerous regulations and permits, such as those from OSHA or environmental agencies. A systematic process is essential to identify, document, and correct unsafe conditions and non-conformities, thereby preventing incidents and protecting employees and the environment.

Key Features
This new capability provides a brand-new inspection management process within EHS. It allows users to centrally create and manage checklists and execute ad-hoc inspections on-site using a mobile device. The functionality is fully integrated with foundational task management for planning and tracking corrective and preventive actions that arise from inspection findings.

 

 

Video#2: Demo planning and execution of EHS inspections

 

Improved operational compliance overview page with new task insight metrics



Business Background
To maintain proactive compliance and mitigate risk, environmental managers and other stakeholders need an analytical dashboard to monitor compliance data. An at-a-glance view of task statuses is essential for tracking the fulfillment of various legal and internal obligations.

Key Features
The operational compliance overview page has been improved with new task insight metrics, providing a richer analytical experience. The update introduces new charts, including graphical overviews of overdue and due tasks by location and a list of upcoming tasks. These new visualizations offer immediate, actionable insights and help stakeholders better monitor and manage compliance-related activities.

 

 

Video#3: Demo Improved operational compliance overview page with new task insight metrics

 

Expansion of UK-specific incident regulatory reporting



Business Background
Businesses operating in the United Kingdom face complex regulatory reporting requirements for incidents, governed by regulations such as Reporting of Injuries, Diseases and Dangerous Occurrences Regulations (RIDDOR) and the Building Safety Act (BSA). Making this process simpler and more reliable is crucial for ensuring timely and accurate compliance.

Key Features
This innovation significantly enhances regulatory reporting for the UK by expanding on RIDDOR functionalities and introducing new capabilities for BSA. The system now automatically determines the applicability of regulations and generates the necessary forms based on the incident's location. It also supports additional regulation-specific data fields to make the reporting process more robust and streamlined.

 

 

Video#4: Demo Expansion of UK-specific incident regulatory reporting



Investigation overview on the investigation object page



Business Background
Following an incident, a thorough investigation is critical for understanding the circumstances and identifying root causes. Investigation teams require a consolidated, high-level overview to effectively track the status and progress of their work, ensuring all activities are completed efficiently.

Key Features
The new investigation object page provides an intuitive and consolidated overview for the investigation team. Key features include a visual timeline of the investigation, a clear list of open and completed activities, the status of root cause analysis, and team responsibilities. This enhancement provides greater visibility and allows for more efficient management of the entire investigation process.

 

 

Video#5: Demo Investigation overview on the investigation object page





Thanks for taking the time to read this blog.

Don’t hesitate to leave a comment and check out our community page to ask your questions and engage with the experts. Follow the PSCC_Enablement tag to stay up to date with our latest blog posts. 

 

More Information

For a more in-depth exploration, please note that the EHS & Product Compliance Infodays will be held in Amsterdam from March 24th to March 25th. You can view the agenda and register here.


️Watch the replays of our SAP Cloud ERP 2602 Early Release Series! 

From January 12 to January 16, 2026, we hosted a series of 22 expert-led live sessions to highlight the exciting innovations shipped with the 2602 release of SAP Cloud ERP. Don't miss this chance and watch them on demand–anywhere, anytime. 

 

Find all our assets 

We at Cloud ERP Product Success offer a service as versatile as our product itself. Check out the numerous offerings our team has created for you below:

entry.title: Hybrid Integration Pattern- SAP S/4HANA Vendor Data to Azure Service Bus Using SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
I am currently working on a project where a Hybrid Integration platform (SAP BTP Integration Suite and Azure Integration Services) is used as middleware between SAP and Non-SAP applications.

Business Requirement:

Transmit new and modified Business Partner (vendor) details from SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to Azure Service Bus using the hybrid integration setup.

In this first part of the blog, we explain how vendor messages generated from SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud SOAP Outbound APIs flow through the integration platform and Azure services.

Introduction:

In this project, we built an integration to send new and updated vendor (Business Partner) data from SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud to Azure Service Bus using SAP Integration Suite as the middleware.

SAP sends the vendor data as a SOAP message, which is processed and converted into XML format in SAP Integration Suite. The message is then published to Azure Service Bus, picked up by Azure Logic Apps, routed through Azure API Management, and finally delivered to the Coupa REST API endpoint.

This integration enables fast, secure, and reliable data exchange between SAP, Azure, and Coupa with minimal delay.

End to End Core Interface Design:

what I worked on as an SAP BTP Integration Suite Developer

Developed integrations between SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud SOAP Outbound APIs and Azure Service Bus using SAP Integration Suite iflows.

Implementation:

Step 1: Configure SOAP Sender Adapter

In this step, we set up the SOAP Sender Adapter so SAP Integration Suite can receive vendor data from SAP S/4HANA. We configure the URL, connect the WSDL, and enable security so SAP can safely send messages into the flow.

Step 2: Receive Vendor Data from SAP S/4HANA (SOAP)

It receives the Business Partner (Vendor) SOAP message sent from SAP S/4HANA and makes the XML payload available for processing.

Step 3: Add Tracking Details to the message

We use this step to track and identify each vendor message as it moves through the integration flow by adding values like vendor Number and Correlation ID.

Step 4: Decode Base64 Payload 

The Base64 Decoder is used to convert the encoded SAP payload into readable XML format. This ensures the data can be mapped and processed correctly in the integration flow.

Step 5: Map Vendor Source Data to Target Format

Here, we connect the SAP vendor fields to the matching fields in the target format so the data is passed correctly and can be understood by the next system.

Step 6:  Add Azure Message Headers

We add Azure-related details like MessageId, ContentType, and Label to the message. These help Azure Service Bus identify the message, track it properly, and process it correctly.

Step 7: Publish Vendor Data to Azure Service Bus Topic

In the step, we send the vendor data from SAP Integration Suite to Azure Service Bus. We connect securely to Azure and publish the message to a topic so it can be picked up and used by other systems.

Connection Tab:

Processing Tab:

What Happens After Data Reaches Azure Service Bus 

Once the vendor data is sent to the Azure Service Bus Topic, it is stored safely and made available for other systems to use. The topic  works like a message hub, where one message can be shared with multiple consumers if needed.

Azure Service Bus ensures reliable processing by handing message retries and moving failed messages to a dead-letter queue instead of losing them. This improves the reliability of integration and reduces rick data loss.

Another key benefit is system decoupling. SAP sends the message once, and downstream systems can process it independently. this improves performance and makes it easier to add new consumers in the future without changing the SAP integration flow.

Note:

In case error happen in the middle of the flow and from SAP, it is better a add a global retry mechanism so the message can be automatically retried. This is a recommended approach, but it is not part of the current blog.

 

In this hybrid integration, my role is to send the vendor data from SAP Integration Suite to Azure Service Bus, making sure the message is delivered safely so other system can pick it up process it further.

Conclusion:

This setup shows how SAP and Azure work together using SAP Integration Suite to send vendor data smoothly. By checking and converting the data before sending it to Azure Service Bus, the flow stays clean and avoids errors. Overall, this approach makes the integration easier to manage, more flexible, and able to handle growing business data needs.

entry.title: Converting to the 3-System Landscape – Part 3: Rapid Conversion Strategies
